nativeRange.habitat: Shallow coral reef flats, lagoons, and seaward reefs; depth range 1–50 m, most commonly 1–20 m

diet.notes: Primarily grazes on macroalgae and microalgae including filamentous algae, nori, spirulina, and seaweed sheets. Has been documented switching from fine-textured filamentous algae to coarser algae as it grows. Accepts dried seaweed clips and herbivore-formulated pellets. A well-fed specimen is advised to prevent coral nipping.

imageCandidates.notes: Rickard Zerpe, 2004x1503. Sharp, well-lit lateral view of a live, in-colour Siganus spinus showing the diagnostic vermiculated 'scribbled' olive-and-white maze pattern; whole fish in frame.
David Burdick, 2592x1944. In-situ reef shot of a large shoal of Siganus spinus over branching coral; shows natural grouping behaviour and the scribbled pattern across many individuals.

Pierre Pericard, 2048x1536. Clean full lateral profile of a single live specimen with reddish maze pattern and pale yellow caudal fin; slightly soft from ambient water but correctly ID'd and complete.

summary: The Little Spinefoot is a venomous-spined herbivore from Indo-Pacific reef flats, generally considered peaceful toward most reef tankmates and valued for its ability to control hair algae. It is generally recommended for established reef systems with consistent access to algae.
careNotes: The Little Spinefoot is generally considered one of the easier rabbitfish to keep, though moderate care is advised due to its size requirements and dietary needs. A tank of roughly 440 L (around 115 gallons) or larger is generally recommended, with ample open swimming space and live rock for grazing. Venomous dorsal and anal fin spines can inflict a painful sting — handling with a net is strongly advised and bare-hand contact should be avoided. Like other members of the family Siganidae (rabbitfish), this species tends to be aggressive toward conspecifics and other rabbitfish; keeping only one per tank is generally recommended unless the system is very large. The species has been reported to adopt a mottled dark camouflage pattern when stressed or sleeping — this is normal and not a sign of illness. Reef safety is conditional: well-fed specimens have been reported to leave corals alone, but underfed fish may nip at LPS (large-polyp stony) and soft corals. Although schools of fewer than ten individuals have been documented in the wild, captive specimens are typically kept singly.
